content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
March 31,
1992
Hal Stocker
Box 366
Challenge,
CA 95925
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No.
A—92-143
Dear Mr.
Stocker:
This letter is in response to your request for advice
regarding the campaign disclosure provisions of the Political Re
form Act
(the “Act
11
) •1
QUESTION
Must an individual, acting as the proponent of a county ref
erendum, file campaign disclosure statements?
CONCLUS ION
Any person or combination of persons who directly or
indirectly receives contributions of $1,000 or more in a calendar
year,
for the purpose of influencing voters,
becomes a committee
and shall retain its status as a committee until such time as that
status is terminated pursuant to Section 84214.
FACTS
You are the proponent of a Yuba County referendum to rescind
an action of the Board of Supervisors.
You are circulating
petitions to gather signatures to qualify the measure for
submission to the Board of Supervisors, which may then submit the
measure to the voters.
You have received some contributions to
finance your efforts to qualify the measure but most of the money
has been your own.
1 /
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000,
et g.
All references to regulations are to Title
2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
4281 Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 9804-O807 • (916)322-566(


Page
2
Stocker,
Hal
ANALYSIS
Section 82013(a)
provides that any person or combination of
persons who directly or indirectly receives contributions totaling
$1,000 or more in a calendar year for the purpose of supporting or
opposing state or local candidates or measures is a “committee.”
Section 82043 defines “measure” as any constitutional
amendment or other proposition which is submitted to a popular
vote at an election by action of a legislative body,
or which is
submitted or is intended to be submitted to a popular vote at an
election by initiative, referendum or recall procedure whether or
not it qualifies for the ballot.
You will not incur campaign disclosure filing obligations
until you have received contributions totaling $1,000 or more in a
calendar year.
To determine if $1,000 has been received,
you must
count all monetary and non—monetary contributions received,
including loans and any personal funds of your own spent in
connection with the qualification or passage of the referendum.
Section 84101(a)
requires every committee qualified under
Section 82013(a)
to file a Statement of Organization
(Form 410)
with the Secretary of State within 10 days after receiving
contributions totaling $1,000 or more in a calendar year.
In
addition to filing the original and one copy of Form 410 with the
Secretary of State, the committee must file a copy with the local
filing officer with whom it will file the originals of its
campaign reports,
which in your case is the Yuba County Registrar
of Voters.
(Section 84215.)
Once the committee qualifies under Section 82013(a), it will
also be required to file periodic campaign reports
(Form 420 or
450).
If it becomes a “committee” during the period in which you
are attempting to qualify the measure, the committee must file a
“post qualification” campaign report due 21 days after the
deadline for filing petitions.
The closing date for the period
covered by this report is seven days prior to the date it is
required to be filed.
(Section 84200.5(f).)
The committee also
will be required to file semi—annual campaign statements each year
and, if the referendum measure qualifies for the ballot,
pre—
election statements must be filed prior to the election.
(Section
84200,
84200.5)
Semi—annual statements are due no later than July 31 for the
period ending June 30,
and no later than January 31 for the period
ending December 31.
(Section 84200.)
The deadlines for pre—
election campaign statements are determined by the date of the
election.
(Section 84200.8.)
Campaign statements must be filed
until the committee is eligible to terminate.
(Sections 82013,


Page
3
Stocker,
Hal
84214.)
The Registrar of Voters for luba County can provide you
with the appropriate forms,
filing dates,
and the Commission’s
campaign information manual
(Manual
D——Campaign Manual for
Committees Primarily Formed to Support or Oppose the
Qualification,
Passage or Defeat of a Ballot Measure).
If you have additional questions, please contact me at
(916)
322—5662.
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel
AJ
By:
Wayne P.
Imberi
Political Reform Consultant

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Must an individual, acting as the proponent of a county ref
erendum, file campaign disclosure statements?

CONCLUSION: Any person or combination of persons who directly or
indirectly receives contributions of $1,000 or more in a calendar
year,
for the purpose of influencing voters,
becomes a committee
and shall retain its status as a committee until such time as that
status is terminated pursuant to Section 84214.
